mn140:1.2: At one time the _Bhagavā_, walking on tour among the Magadhans, arrived at Rājagaha.
mn140:1.3: He went to Bhaggava the potter, and on arrival he said to him:
mn140:10.1: This person, *bhikkhu*, has eighteen mental explorations—
mn140:10.10: This person, *bhikkhu*, has eighteen mental explorations—
mn140:10.11: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn140:10.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:10.3: Having seen a form with the eye, one mentally explores a form that is a basis for joy, one mentally explores a form that is a basis for distress, one mentally explores a form that is a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*];
mn140:10.4: having heard a sound with the ear …
mn140:10.5: having smelled an odor with the nose …
mn140:10.6: having tasted a flavor with the tongue …
mn140:10.7: having touched a tangible with the body …
mn140:10.8: having cognized a *dhamma* with the mind, one mentally explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for joy, one mentally explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for distress, one mentally explores a *dhamma* that is a basis for equanimity [*upekkhā*]—
mn140:10.9: thus there are six explorations of joy, six explorations of distress, and six explorations of equanimity [*upekkhā*].
mn140:11.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:11.3: With discernment [*paññā*] as foundation, with truth as foundation, with relinquishment as foundation, with stilling as foundation.
mn140:11.5: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn140:12.1: One should not neglect discernment [*paññā*], should protect truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only in peace—
mn140:12.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:13.1: And how, *bhikkhu*, does one not neglect discernment [*paññā*]?
mn140:13.3: the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element, the space element, and the consciousness-element [*viññāṇadhātu*].
mn140:14.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the earth element?
mn140:14.2: The earth element may be internal or external.
mn140:14.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal earth element?
mn140:14.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is solid, hard, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*], that is to say—
mn140:14.5: head-hairs, body-hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, feces, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is solid, hard, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*]—
mn140:14.6: this is called the internal earth element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:14.7: Now both the internal earth element and the external earth element are just the earth element.
mn140:14.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:14.9: Seeing it thus as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the earth element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the earth element.
mn140:15.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the water element?
mn140:15.2: The water element may be internal or external.
mn140:15.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal water element?
mn140:15.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is water, watery, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*], that is to say—
mn140:15.5: bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, nasal mucus, lymph, urine, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is water, watery, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*]—
mn140:15.6: this is called the internal water element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:15.7: Now both the internal water element and the external water element are just the water element.
mn140:15.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:15.9: Seeing it thus as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the water element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the water element.
mn140:16.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the fire element?
mn140:16.2: The fire element may be internal or external.
mn140:16.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal fire element?
mn140:16.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is fire, fiery, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*], that is to say—
mn140:16.5: that by which one is warmed, by which one ages, by which one burns, and by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted goes to proper digestion, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is fire, fiery, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*]—
mn140:16.6: this is called the internal fire element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:16.7: Now both the internal fire element and the external fire element are just the fire element.
mn140:16.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:16.9: Seeing it thus as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the fire element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the fire element.
mn140:17.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the air element?
mn140:17.2: The air element may be internal or external.
mn140:17.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal air element?
mn140:17.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is air, airy, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*], that is to say—
mn140:17.5: upward-going winds, downward-going winds, winds in the belly, winds in the intestines, winds moving through the limbs, in-breath, out-breath; or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is air, airy, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*]—
mn140:17.6: this is called the internal air element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:17.7: Now both the internal air element and the external air element are just the air element.
mn140:17.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:17.9: Seeing it thus as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the air element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the air element.
mn140:18.1: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the space element?
mn140:18.2: The space element may be internal or external.
mn140:18.3: And what, *bhikkhu*, is the internal space element?
mn140:18.4: Whatever internally, belonging to oneself, is space, spatial, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*], that is to say—
mn140:18.5: the ear-holes, the nostrils, the mouth-opening, that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted is swallowed, where what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted stands, that by which what is eaten, drunk, chewed, and tasted goes out below, or whatever else internally, belonging to oneself, is space, spatial, a hollow, hollowed, an opening, opened, not touched by flesh and blood, appropriated [*upādinnaṁ*]—
mn140:18.6: this is called the internal space element, *bhikkhu*.
mn140:18.7: Now both the internal space element and the external space element are just the space element.
mn140:18.8: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self’—thus it should be seen as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*].
mn140:18.9: Seeing it thus as it truly is with proper discernment [*sammappaññāya*], one becomes disenchanted with the space element and makes the mind [*citta*] dispassionate toward the space element.
mn140:19.1: Then only consciousness [*viññāṇa*] remains, purified and bright.
mn140:19.10: Dependent on contact that is to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant, *bhikkhu*, neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.11: As one feels neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.12: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant, whatever feeling is born from it—the neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:19.13: Suppose, *bhikkhu*, from the striking together and joining of two sticks, heat is born and fire is produced. And from the very separation and laying aside of those two sticks, the corresponding heat ceases, that subsides.
mn140:19.14: In the same way, *bhikkhu*, dependent on contact that is to be felt as pleasant, pleasant *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.15: As one feels pleasant *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.16: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as pleasant, whatever feeling is born from it—the pleasant *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as pleasant—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:19.17: Dependent on contact that is to be felt as painful, *bhikkhu*, painful *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.18: As one feels painful *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel painful *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.19: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as painful, whatever feeling is born from it—the painful *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as painful—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:19.2: And with that consciousness [*viññāṇa*], what does one cognize?
mn140:19.20: Dependent on contact that is to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant, *bhikkhu*, neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.21: As one feels neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.22: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant, whatever feeling is born from it—the neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as neither-painful-nor-pleasant—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:19.3: One cognizes, ‘pleasant,’ one cognizes, ‘painful,’ one cognizes, ‘neither-painful-nor-pleasant.’
mn140:19.4: Dependent on contact that is to be felt as pleasant, *bhikkhu*, pleasant *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.5: As one feels pleasant *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel pleasant *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.6: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as pleasant, whatever feeling is born from it—the pleasant *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as pleasant—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:19.7: Dependent on contact that is to be felt as painful, *bhikkhu*, painful *vedanā* arises.
mn140:19.8: As one feels painful *vedanā*, one understands: ‘I feel painful *vedanā*.’
mn140:19.9: One understands: ‘With the cessation of that very contact that is to be felt as painful, whatever feeling is born from it—the painful *vedanā* that has arisen dependent on contact to be felt as painful—that ceases, that subsides.’
mn140:2.1: “If it is no inconvenience for you, Bhaggava, I would stay in the workshop for one night.”
mn140:2.2: “It is no inconvenience for me, bhante.
mn140:2.3: But there is a gone-forth one here who arrived earlier to stay.
mn140:2.4: If he allows it, stay as you please, bhante.”
mn140:20.1: Then only equanimity [*upekkhā*] remains, purified and bright, pliant, workable, and radiant.
mn140:20.2: Suppose, *bhikkhu*, a skilled goldsmith or a goldsmith’s apprentice were to set up a furnace, and having set up the furnace were to heat its mouth, and having heated its mouth were to take gold with tongs and put it into the furnace. From time to time he would blow on it, from time to time he would sprinkle it with water, from time to time he would just watch over it. That gold would become well refined, thoroughly refined, rid of flaws, freed from dross, pliant, workable, and radiant. For whatever kind of ornament he might wish—whether a diadem, or earrings, or a neck ornament, or a gold garland—it would serve that purpose for him.
mn140:20.3: In the same way, *bhikkhu*, then only equanimity [*upekkhā*] remains, purified and bright, pliant, workable, and radiant.
mn140:21.2: ‘If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that,
mn140:21.3: then this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that, fed by that [*tadupādānā*], would remain for a long time, for a very long time.
mn140:21.4: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that,
mn140:21.5: then this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that, fed by that [*tadupādānā*], would remain for a long time, for a very long time.
mn140:21.6: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that,
mn140:21.7: then this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that, fed by that [*tadupādānā*], would remain for a long time, for a very long time.
mn140:21.8: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that,
mn140:21.9: then this equanimity [*upekkhā*] of mine, dependent on that, fed by that [*tadupādānā*], would remain for a long time, for a very long time.’
mn140:22.10: He does not construct that, nor does he intend it for *bhava* or for non-*bhava* [*vibhavāya*].
mn140:22.11: Not constructing and not intending for *bhava* or for non-*bhava* [*vibhavāya*], he does not cling/feed [*upādiyati*] to anything in the world.
mn140:22.12: Not clinging/feeding [*anupādiyaṁ*], he does not tremble. Not trembling, he personally attains final *nibbāna*.
mn140:22.13: He understands: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being.’
mn140:22.2: ‘If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of boundless space [*ākāsānañcāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that—
mn140:22.4: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of boundless consciousness [*viññāṇañcāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that—
mn140:22.6: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of nothingness [*ākiñcaññāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that—
mn140:22.8: If I were to direct this equanimity [*upekkhā*], thus purified, thus bright, to the dimension of neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññānāsaññāyatanaṁ*], and were to develop the mind [*citta*] in conformity with that—
mn140:23.1: If he feels pleasant *vedanā*, he understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*].’ He understands: ‘It is not seized on.’ He understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:23.2: If he feels painful *vedanā*, he understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*].’ He understands: ‘It is not seized on.’ He understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:23.3: If he feels neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he understands: ‘It is impermanent [*aniccā*].’ He understands: ‘It is not seized on.’ He understands: ‘It is not delighted in.’
mn140:24.1: If he feels pleasant *vedanā*, he feels it detached from it;
mn140:24.2: if he feels painful *vedanā*, he feels it detached from it;
mn140:24.3: if he feels neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*, he feels it detached from it.
mn140:24.4: As he feels a feeling that ends with the body, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling that ends with the body.’ As he feels a feeling that ends with life, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling that ends with life.’
mn140:24.5: He understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, with the ending of life, all that is felt here, not being delighted in, will grow cool.’
mn140:24.6: Suppose, *bhikkhu*, an oil lamp burns dependent on oil and dependent on a wick.
mn140:24.7: With the exhaustion of that very oil and wick, and with no further supply, lacking fuel it goes out.
mn140:24.8: In the same way, *bhikkhu*, as he feels a feeling that ends with the body, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling that ends with the body.’ As he feels a feeling that ends with life, he understands: ‘I feel a feeling that ends with life.’
mn140:24.9: He understands: ‘With the breakup of the body, after death, with the ending of life, all that is felt here, not being delighted in, will grow cool.’
mn140:25.1: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this supreme foundation of discernment [*paññā*].
mn140:25.2: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the supreme noble discernment [*paññā*], namely—
mn140:25.3: the knowledge of the ending of all *dukkha*.
mn140:26.1: That liberation of his, grounded in truth, is unshakeable.
mn140:26.2: For what has a deceptive nature is false, *bhikkhu*, while what has a non-deceptive nature, *nibbāna*, is truth.
mn140:26.3: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this supreme foundation of truth.
mn140:26.4: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the supreme noble truth, namely—
mn140:27.1: Formerly, for one who did not understand, there were acquisitions that had been fully undertaken and taken up.
mn140:27.2: Those have been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:27.3: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this supreme foundation of relinquishment.
mn140:27.4: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the supreme noble relinquishment, namely—
mn140:28.1: Formerly, for one who did not understand, there was covetousness, desire, and passion.
mn140:28.2: That has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.3: Formerly, for one who did not understand, there was resentment, ill will, and full anger.
mn140:28.4: That has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.5: Formerly, for one who did not understand, there was blindness [*avijjā*] and confusion.
mn140:28.6: That has been abandoned, cut off at the root, made like a palm stump, made into non-being, not liable to arise in the future.
mn140:28.7: Therefore a *bhikkhu* endowed in this way is endowed with this supreme foundation of stilling.
mn140:28.8: For this, *bhikkhu*, is the supreme noble stilling, namely—
mn140:28.9: the stilling of passion, aversion, and delusion.
mn140:29.1: One should not neglect discernment [*paññā*], should protect truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only in peace—
mn140:29.2: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn140:3.1: Now at that time a son of a good family named Pukkusāti had gone forth from the home life to homelessness out of faith in the _Bhagavā_.
mn140:3.2: He was the one who had arrived earlier to stay in that potter’s workshop.
mn140:3.3: Then the _Bhagavā_ went to Venerable Pukkusāti, and on arrival he said to him:
mn140:3.4: “If it is no inconvenience for you, *bhikkhu*, I would stay in the workshop for one night.”
mn140:3.6: Let the venerable one stay as you please.”
mn140:30.1: Where, standing, conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, and when conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, one is called a sage at peace—
mn140:30.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:31.1: ‘I am’—this is a conceiving, *bhikkhu*; ‘I am this’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will not be’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be with *rūpa* [*rūpī*]’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be without *rūpa* [*arūpī*]’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be with *saññā* [*saññī*]’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be without *saññā* [*asaññī*]’—this is a conceiving; ‘I will be with neither *saññā* nor non-*saññā* [*nevasaññīnāsaññī*]’—this is a conceiving.
mn140:31.2: Conceiving, *bhikkhu*, is a disease; conceiving is a tumor; conceiving is a dart.
mn140:31.3: But with the complete going beyond of all conceivings, *bhikkhu*, one is called a sage at peace.
mn140:31.4: And the sage at peace, *bhikkhu*, is not born, does not age, does not die, does not waver, does not long.
mn140:31.5: For there is nothing for him, *bhikkhu*, by which he might be born. Not being born, how could he age? Not aging, how could he die? Not dying, how could he waver? Not wavering, for what would he long?
mn140:32.1: Where, standing, conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, and when conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, one is called a sage at peace—
mn140:32.2: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn140:32.3: Keep in mind this brief analysis of the six elements.”
mn140:33.2: “Surely my teacher has come to me! Surely the one gone to *sukha* has come to me! Surely the perfectly awakened one has come to me!” Rising from his seat, arranging his robe over one shoulder, he bowed with his head at the _Bhagavā_’s feet and said to the _Bhagavā_:
mn140:33.3: “An offense overcame me, bhante, as a fool, as one confused, as one unskillful, in that I thought I should address the _Bhagavā_ with the word ‘friend.’
mn140:33.4: May the _Bhagavā_ accept this offense of mine as an offense, for restraint in the future.”
mn140:33.5: “Indeed, *bhikkhu*, an offense overcame you, as a fool, as one confused, as one unskillful, in that you thought you should address me with the word ‘friend.’
mn140:33.6: But because you see the offense as an offense and make amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, we accept that from you.
mn140:33.7: For this is growth in the noble *vinaya*, *bhikkhu*: when one sees an offense as an offense, makes amends in accordance with the *dhamma*, and undertakes restraint for the future.”
mn140:34.1: “Bhante, may I receive the full ordination in the _Bhagavā_’s presence?”
mn140:34.2: “But is your bowl and robe complete, *bhikkhu*?”
mn140:34.3: “No, bhante, my bowl and robe are not complete.”
mn140:34.4: “*Bhikkhu*, Tathāgatas do not give full ordination to one whose bowl and robe are incomplete.”
mn140:35.1: Then Venerable Pukkusāti, having delighted in and approved of the _Bhagavā_’s words, rose from his seat, paid homage to the _Bhagavā_, circumambulated him keeping his right side toward him, and left to look for a bowl and robe.
mn140:35.2: Then, while Venerable Pukkusāti was wandering in search of a bowl and robe, a stray cow took his life.
mn140:36.1: Then several *bhikkhus* went to the _Bhagavā_. On arrival, having paid homage to the _Bhagavā_, they sat down to one side. Seated to one side, those *bhikkhus* said to the _Bhagavā_:
mn140:36.2: “Bhante, that son of a good family named Pukkusāti, who was instructed by the _Bhagavā_ with a brief instruction, has died.
mn140:36.3: What is his destination? What is his future course?”
mn140:36.4: “Pukkusāti, *bhikkhus*, the son of a good family, was wise. He practiced in accordance with the *dhamma* and did not trouble me over a matter of *dhamma*.
mn140:36.5: With the destruction of the five lower fetters, *bhikkhus*, Pukkusāti, the son of a good family, is one who is spontaneously reborn, there to attain final *nibbāna*, not liable to return from that world.”
mn140:36.7: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the _Bhagavā_’s words.
mn140:36.8: The Analysis of the Elements is finished, the tenth.
mn140:4.1: Then the _Bhagavā_ entered the potter’s workshop, arranged a grass mat at one side, and sat down cross-legged, setting his body erect and establishing *sati* before him.
mn140:4.2: Then the _Bhagavā_ spent most of the night sitting.
mn140:4.3: And Venerable Pukkusāti also spent most of the night sitting.
mn140:4.5: “This son of a good family conducts himself in an inspiring way.
mn140:4.6: What if I were to question him?”
mn140:4.7: Then the _Bhagavā_ said to Venerable Pukkusāti:
mn140:5.1: “For whose sake have you gone forth, *bhikkhu*? Who is your teacher? And whose *dhamma* do you approve of?”
mn140:5.10: That _Bhagavā_, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, is now dwelling there.”
mn140:5.11: “But have you ever seen that _Bhagavā_ before, *bhikkhu*?
mn140:5.12: And if you were to see him, would you recognize him?”
mn140:5.13: “No, friend, I have never seen that _Bhagavā_ before.
mn140:5.14: And if I were to see him, I would not recognize him.”
mn140:5.2: “There is, friend, the ascetic Gotama, a Sakyan son who went forth from a Sakyan clan.
mn140:5.3: About that _Bhagavā_ Gotama this fine report has spread:
mn140:5.4: ‘So too, the _Bhagavā_ is an *arahant*, perfectly awakened, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, one who has gone to *sukha*, knower of the world, unsurpassed trainer of those fit to be trained, teacher of devatās and humans, awakened, the _Bhagavā_.’
mn140:5.5: For the sake of that _Bhagavā_ I have gone forth.
mn140:5.7: It is that _Bhagavā_’s *dhamma* that I approve of.”
mn140:5.8: “But where, *bhikkhu*, is that _Bhagavā_, the *arahant*, the perfectly awakened one, now dwelling?”
mn140:5.9: “There is, friend, in the northern country a city named Sāvatthī.
mn140:6.2: “And this son of a good family has gone forth for my sake.
mn140:6.3: What if I were to teach him *dhamma*?”
mn140:6.6: Listen to it, apply your mind well; I will speak.”
mn140:6.7: “Yes, friend,” Venerable Pukkusāti replied to the _Bhagavā_.
mn140:7.1: “This person, *bhikkhu*, is of six elements, has six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*], eighteen mental explorations, and four foundations.
mn140:7.2: Where, standing, conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, and when conceiving-*āsavas* [*maññassavā*] do not continue, one is called a sage at peace.
mn140:7.3: One should not neglect discernment [*paññā*], should protect truth, should cultivate relinquishment, and should train only in peace—
mn140:7.4: this is the summary of the Analysis of the Elements.
mn140:8.1: This person, *bhikkhu*, is of six elements—
mn140:8.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:8.4: the earth element, the water element, the fire element, the air element, the space element, and the consciousness-element [*viññāṇadhātu*].
mn140:8.5: This person, *bhikkhu*, is of six elements—
mn140:8.6: what was said was said in dependence on this.
mn140:9.1: This person, *bhikkhu*, has six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*]—
mn140:9.2: so it was said. In dependence on what was this said?
mn140:9.3: The dimension of eye-contact [*cakkhusamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of ear-contact [*sotasamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of nose-contact [*ghānasamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of tongue-contact [*jivhāsamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of body-contact [*kāyasamphassāyatanaṁ*], the dimension of mind-contact [*manosamphassāyatanaṁ*].
mn140:9.4: This person, *bhikkhu*, has six dimensions of contact [*phassāyatano*]—
mn140:9.5: what was said was said in dependence on this.
